Want to Start Vibe Coding? The Fundamentals Beginners Should Get Down First

Lately a lot of friends have been asking me, "I want to use AI to build things too — where do I start?" And what they're really asking about is Vibe Coding. This post is about the fundamentals beginners should get down before diving into Vibe Coding — Git, command line, UI Frameworks, and so on. So you don't get halfway through and find you're stuck not knowing how to communicate with the AI.
Which Claude Code Files Should You Commit? CLAUDE.md, Settings, Rules, Skills — A Complete Breakdown

After you start using Claude Code, your project ends up with a bunch of new files — CLAUDE.md, .claude/settings.json, rules, skills, and so on. Which ones should you commit? Which are personal settings that shouldn't be pushed up? This post walks through Claude Code's multi-tier settings architecture and load order, so you understand the line between personal and team.
A Safer Alternative to git push --force: git push --force-with-lease

After using git rebase or git commit --amend to rewrite history, you have to force-push to update the remote. But git push --force overwrites the remote unconditionally — which can blow away your teammates' work. This post introduces a safer alternative: git push --force-with-lease and --force-if-includes, which add a layer of protection before you force-push.
Git init Shows a master Branch Hint? Here's How to Set main as Default

Some of my students were running into a hint that says "master" is set as the initial branch name when they used Git, and they were curious about what was going on. So I wrote a quick post to log this down.
VSCode 'Can't Find Git, Install It or Use git.path' — How to Fix
One day VSCode threw the error "Can't find Git. Install it or use the git.path setting to specify the Git path." Here's the fix when Git is already installed but VSCode can't pick it up.
New Git Commands You Should Start Using
A quick writeup on some newer Git commands I've found genuinely useful and worth sharing.
The Simplest Way to Deploy Your Own gh-pages Site

gh-pages is a free GitHub service that lets you deploy a static site for showcase purposes. For frontend devs that's super handy. But there are a bunch of ways to deploy, so this post walks through the simplest one.
Git Filename Case Sensitivity Issues and How to Fix Them
Git treats readme.md and README.md as the same file on macOS or Windows, so renaming silently does nothing. How to reproduce the trap and two clean ways to fix it.
Migrating From Heroku to Render: A Deployment Guide

Heroku was my go-to backend platform for years, but in 2022/11/28 they killed the free tier. This post is the walkthrough I went through migrating my sites to Render.com — why I picked Render, how to deploy a Node.js project end to end (with environment variables), and the Render-specific quirks worth knowing about (sleep mode, shared monthly hours, slow free-tier deploys, the HTTP endpoint requirement, and bots that send twice).

Ever thought your Git commits look kind of ugly? Or you can't figure out what to write half the time? This post introduces git-cz, a tool that frees you from writing commit messages by hand and makes them prettier. Lazy developer's paradise — not even an exaggeration.
